title: Choosing Falls Prevention Software: 7 Questions to Ask Before You Buy
description: Seven questions to ask before buying falls prevention software. Compare clinical validation, training, and integration.

blocks.question: What criteria should guide your selection of falls prevention software?
blocks.directAnswer: Falls prevention software selection depends on 7 key factors: clinical validation, ease of use, training requirements, health IT integration, reporting capabilities, scalability to your organization size, and cost-benefit analysis. Evaluate vendors against these criteria to avoid purchasing mismatches that frustrate staff and fail to improve outcomes.

tldrBullets.text: Software must use validated falls risk assessment tools, not proprietary algorithms.
User interface design directly affects adoption and staff compliance.
Scalability matters: can the system grow from one site to 50+ locations?

blocks.expandedAnswer: Many falls prevention software products exist, but few meet evidence-based standards. Choosing the wrong system wastes budget, frustrates staff, and fails to improve outcomes. This article outlines 7 procurement questions that separate fit-for-purpose solutions from overcomplicated or poorly validated alternatives. Use this framework to evaluate vendors and negotiate contracts that meet your organization's clinical and operational needs.

sources.title: Health IT Procurement Best Practice Guide

blocks.body:
Question 1: Is the Assessment Tool Clinically Validated?

Ask the vendor: what validated falls risk assessment tool is embedded in your software? Look for tools with published evidence in peer-reviewed literature. Examples include the Morse Fall Scale, Johns Hopkins Fall Risk Assessment Tool, or CDC STEADI instruments. Avoid proprietary algorithms developed by the vendor without independent validation.

Request the vendor's evidence. Ask for peer-reviewed publications validating the tool. Ask about inter-rater reliability and sensitivity/specificity data. A vendor confident in their tool will provide this evidence readily. Validated tools ensure your organization is using evidence-based assessment, reducing liability and improving outcomes.

Question 2: Is the User Interface Intuitive and Designed for Your Workflows?

Poor user interface design is the leading cause of health IT implementation failure. Staff avoid software they find frustrating or slow. Ask the vendor for a demonstration that focuses on workflow. Watch a carer or care assistant administer the assessment. Can they complete it in 10-15 minutes without complex navigation or excessive typing?

Request a free trial or sandbox environment. Have your staff use the software for a week. Gather feedback on ease of use. Ask staff: would you use this daily without complaint? Their answer matters more than vendor marketing claims. Software that takes 30 minutes to complete an assessment will face adoption resistance, no matter how clinically sound the underlying tool.

Question 3: What Training Is Required and How Long Does Competency Take?

Ask the vendor: how much training does staff need before using the software independently? Can non-clinical staff be trained? How long is initial training (in minutes or hours)? What ongoing training is required annually? A system requiring multi-day classroom training before staff can use it will face implementation barriers. A system requiring less than 15 minutes of training enables rapid scale.

Ask about competency verification. Does the vendor provide testing or observation protocols? How does your organization verify that staff are using the system correctly? Ask about train-the-trainer models: can your own clinical leads train new staff, or must the vendor conduct all training? Vendor-dependent training increases costs and slows scaling.

Question 4: Does the Software Integrate With Your Existing Health IT Systems?

Ask the vendor: what electronic health records (EHRs) does your software integrate with? Can screening results automatically populate care plans? Can assessment data feed into quality reporting systems? Poor integration means duplicate data entry. Staff enter falls risk information in your EHR and again in the falls prevention software. This duplication frustrates staff and creates inconsistency.

Understand integration capabilities: does the vendor offer HL7 integration, API connections, or direct HL7 messaging? Or do you need manual data exports and imports? Request a technical assessment with your IT department before purchasing. Integration failures are expensive to remediate post-purchase.

Question 5: What Reports and Dashboards Does the Software Provide?

Ask the vendor: what reports can your organization generate from the software? Can you run reports on screening compliance (what percentage of residents were screened)? Can you identify high-risk cohorts and track falls rates over time? Can you generate reports for quality metrics (HHVBP, VBP, CMS Five-Star)? Can staff access real-time dashboards showing their current caseload risks?

Reporting capability directly impacts your organization's ability to improve. Poor reporting makes it difficult to identify trends, target interventions, or demonstrate quality improvements. Request sample reports from the vendor and evaluate whether they answer your key quality questions. Reporting should be accessible to frontline staff and executives without IT support.

Question 6: Can the System Scale From Your Current Size to Your Growth Plans?

Ask the vendor: can your software support a single location? 10 locations? 50 locations? 100 locations? What architecture limitations exist? Some solutions use on-premise servers and become unmanageable at scale. Others use cloud infrastructure and scale easily. Ask about deployment options: cloud, hybrid, or on-premise?

For multi-site agencies, ask about centralized reporting and governance. Can your corporate office access real-time dashboards across all branch locations? Can you run comparative reports showing which branches screen most effectively? Can you deploy system updates to 50 sites simultaneously? Scalability roadmaps and cloud architecture matter for growing organizations.

Question 7: What Is the Total Cost of Ownership and Return on Investment?

Ask the vendor for transparent pricing: what are initial licensing costs? What are annual support and hosting fees? What costs accrue per user, per location, per assessment, or other metrics? Request a three-year cost forecast. Many vendors quote initial licensing costs but hide substantial annual fees.

Calculate return on investment: if the software reduces falls by 20%, what is the cost savings in prevented injuries, liability, and improved reimbursement? For home health agencies participating in HHVBP, a 1% improvement in quality performance ranking generates $12,500-$20,000 in additional annual Medicare revenue. For SNFs, falls reduction improves Five-Star ratings and increases admissions. For home care agencies, falls prevention reduces insurance claims.

A software solution costing $40,000 annually can generate ROI within 12-18 months if it reduces falls rates by 20-25%. Be wary of solutions with high upfront costs and unclear ongoing expenses. Negotiate pricing based on transparent cost structures and realistic ROI timelines.

stats.stat: Health IT implementation failure rates exceed 40% when staff find software difficult to use.
stats.source: Health IT Adoption and Usability Study

stats.stat: Organizations using validated falls risk tools achieve 25-35% greater falls reduction than those using proprietary algorithms.
Software with strong EHR integration reduces data entry time by 40-50% compared to standalone systems.

items.question: Should we choose software based on vendor reputation or on specific product evaluation?
items.answer: Product evaluation matters more than vendor reputation. A well-known vendor may offer poor usability or limited reporting. Conversely, smaller vendors may offer excellent products. Evaluate each system against the 7 criteria above rather than relying on brand recognition.

items.question: What if our preferred software does not integrate with our EHR?
items.answer: Poor integration creates workflow friction and staff frustration. Either negotiate with the vendor for integration development, or choose software that integrates with your EHR. Custom integration projects are expensive and time-consuming; factor this into cost calculations.

items.question: How do we know if we are choosing the right solution before implementation?
items.answer: Request a pilot period with your staff. Run the software for 2-4 weeks across a small group of users. Collect feedback from frontline staff on usability and workflow fit. Pilot results predict implementation success more accurately than vendor demos.

items.question: What happens if we choose software that fails adoption?
items.answer: Poor adoption leads to wasted investment and missed clinical benefits. Build adoption monitoring into your implementation plan. Track usage rates weekly. If adoption is low after four weeks, investigate reasons: usability issues, workflow mismatch, or insufficient training. Address barriers quickly or consider switching solutions early.

blocks.heading: Evaluate falls prevention solutions against evidence-based criteria
blocks.bodyText: Use these 7 questions to assess any falls prevention software. Learn how Able Assess meets these criteria: validated assessment tools, 15-minute training, seamless EHR integration, real-time reporting, and transparent ROI.
